concepts.title: Smooth projective curves and their genus
concepts.blurb: A smooth projective curve over an algebraically closed field $k$ is a smooth projective integral $k$-scheme of dimension 1. Its genus $g$ is the dimension of $H^0(C, \Omega^1_C)$ — over $\mathbb{C}$ this matches the topological genus of the Riemann surface $C(\mathbb{C})$.

concepts.title: Divisors on curves
concepts.blurb: A divisor $D = \sum n_p [p]$ is a finite formal $\mathbb{Z}$-sum of points. The degree is $\deg D = \sum n_p$, and $\mathrm{Pic}^d(C)$ classifies linear-equivalence classes of degree-$d$ divisors. $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$ is the Jacobian — an abelian variety of dimension $g$.

concepts.title: Riemann–Roch on curves
concepts.blurb: For a divisor $D$ on a smooth projective curve of genus $g$, $h^0(D) - h^0(K - D) = \deg D - g + 1$, where $K$ is the canonical divisor. Riemann–Roch is the curve-level explanation of why genus controls the dimension of linear systems.

concepts.title: The canonical embedding
concepts.blurb: If $g \ge 2$ and $C$ is non-hyperelliptic, the canonical divisor $K$ embeds $C \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^{g-1}$ as a curve of degree $2g - 2$. The image is the canonical model — the most natural projective realization of an abstract curve.

concepts.title: Hyperelliptic curves
concepts.blurb: A curve of genus $g \ge 2$ is hyperelliptic if it admits a degree-2 map to $\mathbb{P}^1$. Equivalently, $|K|$ does not separate points. Hyperelliptic curves form a codimension-$(g-2)$ locus in $\mathcal{M}_g$ — most curves of genus $\ge 3$ are not hyperelliptic.

concepts.title: Moduli of higher-genus curves $\mathcal{M}_g$
concepts.blurb: $\mathcal{M}_g$ is the moduli space (DM stack) of smooth projective curves of genus $g$. For $g \ge 2$ it has dimension $3g - 3$; the Deligne–Mumford compactification $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ adds stable nodal curves at the boundary.
